b' Money Stuff
Yeesh! UBS Group AG is planning to cut more than half of Credit Suisse Group AG\xe2\x80\x99s 45,000-strong workforce starting next month as a result of

UBS vs. CS

Yeesh !

UBS Group AG is planning to cut more than half of Credit Suisse Group AG\xe2\x80\x99s 45,000-strong workforce starting next month as a result of the bank\xe2\x80\x99s emergency takeover.

Bankers, traders and support staff in Credit Suisse\xe2\x80\x99s investment bank in London, New York, and in some parts of Asia are expected to bear the brunt of the cuts, with almost all activities at risk, people familiar with the matter said.

Staffers have been told to expect three rounds of cuts this year, with the first expected by the end of July and two more rounds tentatively planned for September and October, the people added, asking not to be named as the plans aren\xe2\x80\x99t public.

Three months after UBS agreed to buy Credit Suisse in a government-brokered rescue, the full extent of the job cuts is starting to become clear. UBS, whose combined workforce jumped to about 120,000 when the deal closed, has said it aims to save some $6 billion in staff costs in the coming years.

UBS intends to ultimately reduce the total combined headcount by about 30%, or 35,000 people, two of the people said. That\xe2\x80\x99s broadly in line with an overall reduction of around 30,000 estimated by analysts at Redburn in a report on UBS this month.

More than half! One way to think about the financial-services business is that many jobs involve\xc2\xa0some combination of marketing and execution. If you are a mergers-and-acquisitions banker, part of your job is to advise companies on mergers, structure and negotiate deals, etc.; another part of your job is going around to companies and pitching them on why they should hire you , rather than another bank, to advise them on their mergers. In much of finance these two roles are combined in one job: The banker who leads the M&A pitch is also the banker who will lead the deal team; the bond salesperson who takes you out for drinks also fills your bond order; the private wealth manager who cold-calls you and asks\xc2\xa0to manage your money also picks investments for you. What banks are selling is expertise and relationships, and it\xe2\x80\x99s harder to sell expertise and relationships if you\xe2\x80\x99re not\xc2\xa0the expert who will have the relationship. But they are different parts of the job, and the bank needs to hire enough people to do both.

If some big investment bank magically vanished, somebody else \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0some other bankers at some other bank \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0would presumably have to take over its execution work. There is external demand for that work: Companies do want mergers, institutions do want to trade bonds, private wealth does want to be managed, etc., and if a bank vanished its customers would have to go elsewhere for those services. Presumably all the bankers at all the other banks are reasonably busy already, executing their own work, and if a big bank vanished then those other banks would have to hire more bankers to take on the additional execution work.

But nobody would necessarily need to take over the vanished bank\xe2\x80\x99s marketing work. Companies want to do mergers, so they sit through pitches from all the leading banks and pick the best one to do their mergers; they will be fine with one less pitch. When a bank vanishes, its competitors can invest less in marketing, because they have one less competitor to market against. In particular, if there are two big Swiss banks, they are going to be pitching against each other a lot, and they will each have to employ a bunch of skilled and well-connected bankers to win mandates in head-to-head competition. If there is one big Swiss bank, that whole problem goes away! You just show up and say \xe2\x80\x9cI hear you want a merger, I can execute it for you, and no one else can\xe2\x80\x9d; it\xe2\x80\x99s all very tidy.

You could imagine a scenario in which UBS bought Credit Suisse and did no headcount reductions: It might look around and say \xe2\x80\x9call of our bankers are busy all day doing work that brings in more money than they cost, and all of their bankers are busy all day doing work that brings in more money than they cost, and all of that work is additive, so we\xe2\x80\x99re going to keep everybody.\xe2\x80\x9d

You could imagine a scenario in which UBS bought Credit Suisse and fired more than everybody at Credit Suisse: It might look around and say \xe2\x80\x9clook, we have good automated systems for trading stocks and putting private-wealth clients in index funds, now that rates are higher our M&A teams have plenty of slack to do more due diligence, and it turns out that there\xe2\x80\x99s no marginal cost to doubling the work that we actually do for clients. So\xc2\xa0we\xe2\x80\x99ll just have our UBS people and systems provide all the services that Credit Suisse was providing its clients,\xc2\xa0fire all of the Credit Suisse people, and also fire our people who were in charge of trying to lure clients from Credit Suisse, since we don\xe2\x80\x99t have to do that anymore.\xe2\x80\x9d

The actual answer is apparently halfway in between? That is a slight exaggeration; one thing that happened is that a lot of the busiest Credit Suisse teams have already moved elsewhere:

While UBS had originally planned to keep the top 20% of dealmakers, in particular those focusing on technology, media and telecoms, many of the top performing bankers have already departed or been poached by competitors, people said. Deutsche Bank AG, Jefferies Financial Group Inc. and Wells Fargo & Co. are among competitors who have snapped up Credit Suisse staff in recent months.

UBS is hoping to retain the majority of Credit Suisse\xe2\x80\x99s private bankers, though many have already left, two of the people said.

By the way, this model is\xc2\xa0oversimplified. In particular, in risk businesses at investment banks, some execution work is not worth doing: If you are trading derivatives that reliably lose money, or making loans to clients who are not creditworthy, you should just stop doing that. In some ways this is a form of marketing: A good way for an investment bank to gain market share is to do trades with negative risk-adjusted returns, because lots of people will want to be on the other side of those trades.\xc2\xa0The team at Credit Suisse that gave synthetic prime brokerage financing to Archegos should just stop, and it did . One way for UBS to save on headcount is by no longer having to compete with Credit Suisse for deals. Another way for UBS to save on headcount is by no longer doing whole categories of deals that Credit Suisse did (the ones that blew up Credit Suisse). The less competition there is among big banks, the less any bank will feel the need to take dumb risks to win business.

Oh Lordstown

In about 2020, a lot of electric-vehicle companies went public in the US and raised money by merging with special-purpose acquisition companies. Some general features of these de-SPAC deals were:

  • The EV company was not all that far along in developing a commercially viable vehicle.
  • But it gave shareholders projections saying that it would sell a zillion vehicles at huge profits by, like, 2023.
  • It did not.

It is interesting to consider how that might end. Like, you raise a bunch of money from retail investors to build electric vehicles, you flail around for a while not building electric vehicles \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0then what? Some options:

  1. You keep flailing until you run out of money, then stop. You file for bankruptcy, there\xe2\x80\x99s no money left, there\xe2\x80\x99s no debt (who would lend you money?), the shareholders get zero, it\xe2\x80\x99s not great but it\xe2\x80\x99s fairly straightforward.
  2. You stop flailing at some point before you run out of money. You file for bankruptcy,\xc2\xa0there is money left, there\xe2\x80\x99s no debt (who would lend you money?), you sell whatever assets there are, and you divide up whatever money is left among the shareholders. This is a better outcome than No. 1, though perhaps less likely. If you run a company, it hasn\xe2\x80\x99t succeeded in making electric vehicles, and it hasn\xe2\x80\x99t run out of money, you will be tempted to keep spending the money to try to make electric vehicles, even if your chances of success are slim. Quitting while there is money left and giving it back to the shareholders is better for them, but psychologically difficult for you.
  3. Same as No. 2, but with a subtle distinction. You don\xe2\x80\x99t have debt per se (who would lend you money?), but you do have some big liabilities. The liabilities are: The people who bought your stock in the de-SPAC deal, who believed your projections about selling a zillion vehicles at huge profits, probably sued you at some point after you started missing projections and the stock started dropping. There is a securities-fraud shareholder class action. The US Securities and Exchange Commission also probably took an interest in those projections. You file for bankruptcy, you have some assets, you sell them for some money, and then a court has to figure out (1) how much of the money goes to the SEC, (2) how much goes to the (current and former)\xc2\xa0shareholders who\xc2\xa0sued you for fraud and (3) how much is left over for your\xc2\xa0current shareholders.

So here is the August 2020 investor presentation from electric-pickup-truck company Lordstown Motors Corp.\xe2\x80\x99s de-SPAC merger, projecting that it would be in \xe2\x80\x9cfull production\xe2\x80\x9d by the second half of 2021, that it would deliver 31,600 vehicles in 2022 and 65,000 in 2023, and that it \xe2\x80\x9chas already received ~27k orders (average order size of ~300 trucks) before the first vehicle has been produced, representing potential revenue sufficient to cover 2021 production and into 2022.\xe2\x80\x9d In fact Lordstown has produced\xc2\xa065 trucks \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0not 65,000, just 65 \xe2\x80\x94 in its entire history through yesterday, and its former chief executive officer resigned in 2021 when it turned out that many of those pre-orders were fake .

But Lordstown did manage to raise about $780 million from investors in the SPAC and from institutional investors in a parallel private investment in public equity deal. It spent a surprisingly large amount of that money \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and of other cash that it raised, including by selling a factory to Foxconn Technology Group and getting some equity investment from Foxconn \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0on building its 65 trucks; its losses from operations over the last three years total $890 million, or about $13.7 million per truck. (\xe2\x80\x9cAnticipated [bill of materials]\xc2\xa0costs were approximately $42,000 per vehicle for manufacturing in 2021,\xe2\x80\x9d says Lordstown , which \xe2\x80\x9ccompared favorably to the Company\xe2\x80\x99s anticipated selling price of $52,500 for the Endurance.\xe2\x80\x9d) But it still has money left: As of yesterday, it \xe2\x80\x9chas approximately $136 million in cash on hand\xe2\x80\x9d and no funded debt.

But yesterday Lordstown filed for bankruptcy, and I have been quoting from the first-day declaration in that case. There is no debt to restructure, so the bankruptcy is not about that. There are some assets to sell \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0its December 2022 balance sheet shows $194 million of property, plant and equipment \xe2\x80\x94 and Lordstown plans to run a sale process for its business, but don\xe2\x80\x99t get too excited:

The Company does not believe that capital from other sources is available. In September 2021, the Company retained Jefferies LLC (\xe2\x80\x9cJefferies\xe2\x80\x9d) to explore all market alternatives. Through that process, the Company and Jefferies, in consultation with Foxconn, prepared a list of more than 50 potential investors and strategic\xc2\xa0OEM partners across the globe to be contacted. Jefferies, the Company and Foxconn allocated responsibility to contact the potential investors and strategic OEM partners based upon who had the best relationship with the most relevant person at those companies. While the Company, its advisors, and potential partners held numerous meetings and in-person evaluations of the Endurance, as of the Petition Date, the Company has not received any actionable indications of interest.

There is some operating business left, but it loses money, so Lordstown plans to stop:

Recognizing that it would still require substantial additional funding to execute its business plan, including scaling the Endurance program and developing new vehicle programs, the Company has continued to take steps to reduce its cash burn and preserve its assets for the benefit of stakeholders. Given the Company\xe2\x80\x99s current prospects\xe2\x80\x94including Foxconn\xe2\x80\x99s unwillingness to honor its funding and product development commitments and the extremely limited ability to raise capital in the current market environment\xe2\x80\x94continuing production of the Endurance would only eat away at the Company\xe2\x80\x99s resources. \xe2\x80\xa6

The Company is currently in the process of finalizing a limited number of Endurances still in production. Once that process is complete, the Company intends to halt production of the Endurance until a value-maximizing transaction is identified in order to preserve its assets and turn its focus entirely to maximizing recoveries through the chapter 11 cases.

Schematically this is a company with about $136 million of cash, some equipment and inventory to sell, no debt, and no operating business. It can just stop, send the shareholders their pro rata share of the cash, and go home.

Well, no, there are two real issues in the bankruptcy. One is that yesterday\xc2\xa0Lordstown also sued Foxconn, in the bankruptcy court, essentially blaming Foxconn for its failure. In 2021 Lordstown sold its factory to Foxconn and entered into a series of agreements to work together to make electric trucks; Foxconn would supply the components, while Lordstown would supply \xe2\x80\x9ccross-functional expertise in designing, engineering and developing electric vehicles from concept through launch\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9cadditional capabilities in sales, marketing.\xe2\x80\x9d Lordstown\xe2\x80\x99s current complaint accuses Foxconn of slow-walking and breaching those agreements. [1] (Foxconn vehemently disagrees .)

There is also an investment agreement in which Foxconn agreed to buy about $170 million of Lordstown stock; it bought some but not\xc2\xa0all of that stock, and honestly I am sympathetic? I also would not want to buy stock in a company that was sliding into bankruptcy; if I had an agreement to buy that stock, I would look very carefully at the agreement to see if I could find an escape hatch. [2] But arguably Foxconn's failure to buy the stock is what tipped Lordstown into bankruptcy. Anyway Lordstown is suing over all of this, and I suppose if it wins then it will get either (1) a pile of money to give to shareholders or (2) a better business deal with Foxconn that it can leverage to sell itself for\xc2\xa0more money to give to shareholders.

But the other issue is the liabilities: Lordstown has no debt, but oh does it have lawsuits. Shareholders who believed Lordstown\xe2\x80\x99s hype in 2020 had started suing for securities fraud by 2021, and the SEC started an investigation. [3] \xe2\x80\x9cThe excitement about [Lordstown\xe2\x80\x99s]\xc2\xa0innovative technology and EV aspirations drove its equity market capitalization to reach $5.3 billion,\xe2\x80\x9d says the bankruptcy filing, somewhat euphemistically; its market cap yesterday was about $36 million. If you got excited about Lordstown\xe2\x80\x99s innovative technology and EV aspirations because of its optimistic manufacturing plans and fake pre-orders, so you bought the stock at a $5.3 billion valuation and then saw it go down 99%, you might sue for fraud, and a class of Lordstown shareholders did.

The bankruptcy declaration says:

The purpose of these Chapter 11 Cases is for the Company to maximize value by conducting a robust sale process, reducing its cash burn to preserve resources, and centralizing the claims asserted against the Company to efficiently resolve those claims and provide meaningful recoveries to its creditors and, if sufficient, its shareholders.

As in any bankruptcy, one goal is to maximize the value of assets, by selling the company for as much as it can get and by suing Foxconn for damages. And as in any bankruptcy, the other goal is to figure out who has a claim to the assets, and divide the assets fairly among those claimants. Here the claims are fairly simple, with no debt and limited operations. The question is how much of the money Lordstown has to pay to its former shareholders (or the SEC) for getting their hopes up, and how much money it will have left to pay its current shareholders.

Tokens

The current\xc2\xa0regulatory situation for crypto in the US is that the US Securities and Exchange Commission thinks that basically all crypto tokens (except Bitcoin and a few others) are securities, it keeps arguing this in court, and it likes its chances of winning. If the SEC is right, this poses all sorts of problems for crypto. Two big ones that we have discussed a lot recently are:

  1. New crypto projects that want to raise money by issuing tokens have to either register them with the SEC , which is somewhere between difficult and impossible for token issuers, or sell them under an exemption from registration , which means roughly \xe2\x80\x9csell them only to venture capitalists, institutions and other accredited investors (or non-US investors), and lock up those investors so they don\xe2\x80\x99t dump them on retail.\xe2\x80\x9d And existing crypto projects that previously raised money by selling tokens might get in trouble (and have to pay fines or give back the money) for doing illegal securities offerings.
  2. Crypto exchanges that want to list tokens (other than a few non-securities like Bitcoin) need to\xc2\xa0register with the SEC as national securities exchanges, which is somewhere between difficult and impossible for crypto exchanges. Though there are exceptions here too ; a brokerage that intermediates securities trades for its customers might be able to provide a trading venue without being a full-blown exchange, though it will still have to register with the SEC and be subject to some oversight.

Let\xe2\x80\x99s say that you solve Problem 2, though: You register some trading venue with the SEC (as a national securities exchange or alternative trading system or just a brokerage), and you are allowed to trade tokens that are securities. You still have Problem 1: All the securities that you want to trade are \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0illegal? Illegal-ish? Were issued in unregistered securities offerings?

Well, that strikes me as a fairly manageable problem. Crudely oversimplifying, the rule in the US is that if you sell securities to accredited venture capitalists and they immediately turn around and dump them on retail investors, that\xe2\x80\x99s bad, that\xe2\x80\x99s a public offering of securities and you and the VCs get in trouble. But if you sell the securities to VCs and lock them up for a year, and at the end of the year the VCs dump them on retail, that\xe2\x80\x99s fine, that\xe2\x80\x99s allowed: You sold the securities under an exemption from registration (just to the VCs), and they sold the securities in a regular secondary-market transaction that doesn\xe2\x80\x99t need to be registered.

Again I am oversimplifying the rule, but it is\xc2\xa0Rule 144 , and it is kind of the standard for how crypto-offerings-that-might-be-securities are conducted in the US these days: You sell to VCs with a long lockup, so that the securities offering is exempt. Also it probably helps with trading. The Wall Street Journal reports :

A small brokerage firm thinks it has a blueprint to bring crypto trading into the regulated market.

Prometheum\xe2\x80\x99s plan involves the use of an exemption that U.S. regulators created 50 years ago to permit trading of shares that were restricted, such as those given as compensation to a corporate insider or sold to an early investor. The exemption is used daily in the stock market to sell millions of shares.

It has never been used in crypto, however, which developed as an unregulated alternative to Wall Street that still doesn\xe2\x80\x99t have a federal market overseer. Prometheum, a six-year-old startup with no digital-asset trading revenue, has cast its lot with regulators hoping to move crypto onto regulated exchanges and brokerages. ...

Finding a registered broker who wants to trade crypto assets still doesn\xe2\x80\x99t make the tokens themselves free to be traded. That is because their sale was never registered, making their resale restricted just like the shares given as compensation to CEOs or public-company directors.

Prometheum says the exemption, known as Rule 144, is one way to solve that. ...

Some securities lawyers question whether Rule 144 can be used to trade cryptocurrencies that have been trading on crypto exchanges that don\xe2\x80\x99t follow SEC rules. Meeting the exemption\xe2\x80\x99s requirements, including proving that the seller held the asset for at least a year, can be difficult to establish in crypto where trading was meant to be anonymous, said David Adams, an attorney at Goodwin Procter LLP.

\xe2\x80\x9cAny broker-dealer that embarks down this path is going to be under incredible scrutiny,\xe2\x80\x9d Adams said. \xe2\x80\x9cJust because you are approved\xe2\x80\xa6that does not mean that goodwill will necessarily continue if you start to list some crypto assets or tokens that the SEC\xe2\x80\x9d doesn\xe2\x80\x99t agree with, he said.

This is not quite as simple as \xe2\x80\x9ceven if crypto tokens are illegal securities offerings, if you wait a year they become more legal,\xe2\x80\x9d but that is a little bit what is happening here.

Art vs. fraud

Here is a fun art project:

  1. I go around on social media saying that I have a clever hedge fund that makes returns of 420% per year by trading at nighttime in a multibillion-dollar secret market run by foreign central banks and the Illuminati. [4]
  2. I tell people that if they send me their money I can get those 420% returns for them.
  3. You send me your money.
  4. I keep it.
  5. You complain.
  6. I say \xe2\x80\x9cno no no, this was an art project, I am pointing out the contradictions in global capitalism by showing the wild stuff that people believe.\xe2\x80\x9d
  7. You say \xe2\x80\x9cwell, fine, but give me my money back.\xe2\x80\x9d
  8. I say \xe2\x80\x9cno keeping your money is what makes it art.\xe2\x80\x9d

Does this work? No? But I have been obsessed with the story of MetaBirkins, in which a guy sold digital pictures of what looked like Herm\xc3\xa8s Birkin bags to gullible crypto/non-fungible-token investors. Herm\xc3\xa8s sued him for copyright infringement, and he argued, no, art project! \xe2\x80\x9cArtistic experiment that examines how society places value on status symbols.\xe2\x80\x9d\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x9cMy MetaBirkins project as a whole was an artistic experiment to explore where the value in the Birkin handbag actually lies\xe2\x80\x94in the handcrafted physical object, or in the image it projects?\xe2\x80\x9d\xc2\xa0I wrote : \xe2\x80\x9cIn some sense anyone who does any scam is running an artistic experiment that examines how much money people are willing to give them, but that is not a defense!\xe2\x80\x9d And: \xe2\x80\x9cYou should hack his crypto wallet and steal all the money he got for the MetaBirkins, and then say \xe2\x80\x98well that too was an artistic exploration of where the value actually lies, and I found it, and now it lies in my wallet.\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x9d

Anyway last week the guy lost in court :

Judge Jed Rakoff, who presided over the case in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York, ordered Rothschild and his associates to abstain from minting or selling MetaBirkins NFTs or using online domains associated with the collection. He also doubled down on a jury\xe2\x80\x99s findings that Mason Rothschild, the collection\xe2\x80\x99s creator, attempted to mislead consumers about his digital art pieces\xe2\x80\x99 connection to Hermes\xe2\x80\x99 luxury bags.

\xe2\x80\x9cAs the jury expressly found, Rothschild, a self-described \xe2\x80\x98marketing strategist,\xe2\x80\x99 purposely sought (with some success) to confuse consumers into believing that his non-fungible tokens (\xe2\x80\x98NFTs'), labeled \xe2\x80\x98MetaBirkins\xe2\x80\xa6\xe2\x80\x99 were affiliated with Herm\xc3\xa8s\xe2\x80\x99 iconic \xe2\x80\x98Birkin\xe2\x80\x99 trademarks,\xe2\x80\x9d Judge Rakoff wrote in a filing on Friday.

He added, \xe2\x80\x9cIn effect, the jury found that Rothschild was simply a swindler.\xe2\x80\x9d

I mean! A con artist, like a regular artist, is in the business of creating an imaginary world and inviting his audience to share it. I suppose the difference is that a regular artist\xe2\x80\x99s audience knows that the work is imaginary and willingly suspends disbelief, while the con artist\xe2\x80\x99s audience thinks that the work is real and is tricked into suspending disbelief. But sometimes it is hard to tell which is which just from\xc2\xa0the work itself.

Things happen

The New M&A Rules That Would Delay Million-Dollar Deals. US banks step up sales of loan portfolios to private lenders . Hedge Fund Manager Chris Brown Is Coming Out \xe2\x80\x94 and Fighting Back. Sequoia Made a Fortune Investing in the U.S. and China;\xc2\xa0Then It Had to Pick One. Andurand\xe2\x80\x99s Oil Hedge Fund Plunge Exceeds 50% in Worst Ever Loss. New Carbon Offset Rules Aim to Clean Up Company Climate Claims. Sam Bankman-Fried Loses Bid for Dismissal of Criminal Charges. How to Read an S-1 .\xc2\xa0Rolex and Patek Prices Fall as Subdial Index Nears Two-Year Low. How Tulsa cops brought down a $500 million catalytic converter crime ring . How Frog and Toad became queer anti-capitalist cottagecore icons.

If you'd like to get\xc2\xa0Money\xc2\xa0Stuff\xc2\xa0in handy email form, right in your inbox, please subscribe at this link . Or you can subscribe to Money Stuff and other great Bloomberg newsletters here . Thanks!

[1] It also notes that \xe2\x80\x9cthe JV Agreement contemplated that the JV\xe2\x80\x99s first vehicle program would be based on certain vehicle designs that a Foxconn affiliate in Taiwan, called Foxtron, had already largely developed,\xe2\x80\x9d but that Foxconn ended up not sharing those designs with Lordstown. I am not sure how that fits with Lordstown supplying the cross-functional expertise in designing electric vehicles from scratch.

[2] Foxconn\xe2\x80\x99s escape hatch was that Lordstown\xe2\x80\x99s stock started trading below $1, and Nasdaq sent it a notice saying that it would be delisted if it couldn\xe2\x80\x99t get its stock back up. (Which it did with a reverse stock split.) The investment agreement included a representation that Lordstown\xe2\x80\x99s stock was listed on Nasdaq and that Lordstown had not \xe2\x80\x9creceived any written notification that the SEC or the Nasdaq is contemplating terminating such registration or listing.\xe2\x80\x9d Foxconn terminated the agreement on the grounds that the Nasdaq notice breached that rep; Lordstown argues that it didn\xe2\x80\x99t, and that anyway if it did it was Foxconn\xe2\x80\x99s fault.

[3] "The Company timely tendered claims with respect to the SEC Investigation and Securities Actions to insurers under the applicable directors and officers (\xe2\x80\x98D&O\xe2\x80\x99) policies,\xe2\x80\x9d says the bankruptcy declaration. \xe2\x80\x9cHowever, the Debtors\xe2\x80\x99 primary and excess insurers under the post-SPAC Merger D&O policy have taken the position that no coverage is available for the vast majority of the Securities Actions and the SEC Investigation. The Company and its legal advisors have been and continue to contest the insurers\xe2\x80\x99 denial.\xe2\x80\x9d

[4] If you have not read Guy Lawson\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9cOctopus,\xe2\x80\x9d you should fix that. The secret Illuminati night market is pretty close to the actual theory of the \xe2\x80\x9cprime bank\xe2\x80\x9d scam, and it keeps working.
